title: Spandau Berlin: Quick Summary
items: **Berlin does have an old town, and it is in Spandau.** The district got its town charter in 1232, five years before Berlin’s first documented mention. A stretch of the 14th-century town wall still stands at full height, and the street plan is genuinely medieval.

**The Citadel is a complete Renaissance star fort.** Built 1559 to 1594 and never destroyed, with the 13th-century Juliusturm, the oldest building in Berlin, inside its walls. Open Friday to Wednesday 10:00 to 17:00 and Thursday 13:00 to 20:00; admission 4.50 euros covers museums, exhibitions and the tower.

**One ticket, zone B.** All of Spandau is inside Berlin fare zone B, so a normal AB ticket covers the whole trip. Take the U7 to Zitadelle or Altstadt Spandau, or the S3/S9 to Berlin-Spandau station in about 30 minutes from Alexanderplatz.

**Plan around two opening quirks.** On Thursdays the Citadel opens at 13:00, and on Sundays the free Gotisches Haus, Berlin’s oldest town house and the local tourist office, opens at 12:00. St. Nikolai church is usually open around midday.

**Mind the 2026 construction weekends.** On 24 to 27 July and 31 July to 3 August the Stadtbahn S-Bahn is suspended from Friday evening; the U7 is the reliable way to Spandau on those dates. Long-distance trains skip Berlin-Spandau station for much of 2026, but that does not affect a sightseeing trip.

**Skip the prison pilgrimage.** Spandau Prison was demolished in 1987 and nothing of it remains. The half-day that works: Citadel first, then the old town across the bridge, then the quiet Kolk lanes along the medieval wall.
